summary.en: Madrid’s 'old town' and Mediterranean quarter, this is the beating bohemian heart of the city and ground zero for its famous tapas culture.
A strictly middle-class residential slice of everyday local life, largely untouched by the tourist gaze, showing how everyday Madrileños live.
A fascinating urban juxtaposition, combining the historic writers' quarter with Madrid's most cosmopolitan, hippy, and multicultural district.
The city's hip, alternative epicenter, born from the 1980s counter-cultural movement and now a paradise for vintage fashion and underground clubs.
Madrid's wealthiest and most expensive neighborhood, a grid-like district of upscale boutiques, high-end commerce, and pristine, elegant streets.
The frenetic, symbolic core of the city, where peeling back the layers reveals historic Habsburg architecture and old-school local haunts.

description.en: A famously narrow, historic street that represents the architectural footprint of old Madrid's drinking culture with the highest concentration of tapas taverns.
An architectural oddity and Spain's main Catholic church, whose construction began in the 19th century and finished in the 1990s.
The sprawling streets that host Madrid’s biggest historical flea market, a chaotic, centuries-old maze of commerce and grit.
A highly popular tapas spot renowned for its vibrant energy and mandatory tortilla orders, offering the quintessential loud, authentic Madrid experience.
An understated, elegant, and satisfyingly fun bar dedicated to the local pastime of drinking vermouth, with a genuinely cool, refined vibe.
The legendary spot where actual residents go to debate and devour Spain's most famous dish: the tortilla de patata.
A deep cut for urbanists, this is the brain center managing the second-largest metro network in Europe, Madrid's transit network.
A dedicated tapas and vermouth bar, considered the absolute best place to explore vermut in all its distinct forms—from fruity to bitter.
A highly localized tapas joint in the nearby Quintana area, perfect for trying unique, unusual tapas you won't find at standard downtown joints.
A bright, inviting Georgian restaurant on the Chueca border where you can feast on incredible bread bowls filled with cheese and a dripping egg.
Madrid's northbound arterial road, which offers a stark contrast to the old town and is lined with the city's tallest modern high-rise buildings.
The stadium of Real Madrid. Even from the outside, it is a colossal piece of architecture and the 'ultimate football temple in Europe'.
An Atlas Obscura-style hidden gem where the 19th-century track area of the rail station has been converted into a massive indoor jungle with a turtle pond.
A historic cultural venue that features one of the best and most famous rooftop views in the entire city, overlooking the Gran Vía.
The main artery of the literary quarter, where you can walk the exact paths of Spain's Golden Age writers like Cervantes.
Located inside a working market, this spot serves Spanish tapas infused with Asian and South American flavors, a perfect culinary representation of the neighborhood.
An ultra under-the-radar wine bar hidden in the neighborhood, a spot where true Madrileños engage in the citywide pastime of drinking in hidden spots.
A historic tapas bar open for over 125 years, remaining untouched by modern gentrification and looking like an authentic 'bullfighter’s dream bar'.
A cozy spot and beloved local hideout specializing in Shanghai-style dumplings and Chinese comfort food, perfect for a break from tapas.
A prime thoroughfare for independent design and street art, representing a novel shopping concept taken over by the city's avant-garde community.
The historical heart of the Malasaña district and the ultimate spot for people-watching the city's alternative youth culture.
An incredible historical oddity: an authentic ancient Egyptian temple moved here block-by-block from the Aswan High Dam.
The monumental statue in El Retiro Park, a specific location where locals gather for impromptu drum circles on summer evenings.
An incredible Iranian and Persian spot offering a unique departure from Spanish food, strictly open only for breakfast and lunch to serve local workers.
The flagship store of the luxury brand founded in Madrid in the 19th century, a genuine piece of the city's high-fashion history.
An independent specialty chocolate cafe that is the premier place for extra crispy, sugary churros and thick hot chocolate.
A massive, monumental neoclassical gate commissioned to modernize the city, serving as a grand historic entrance.
A definitively old-school bar where you go for one highly specific local reason: to eat their famous fried codfish.
The iconic, historic statue of the Bear and the Strawberry Tree, which serves as the official symbol of Madrid.
A hidden-in-plain-sight tapas spot near the highly touristy Plaza Mayor that is a rare gem for a leisurely, high-quality lunch.
A small plaque on the ground in the Puerta del Sol that marks the absolute geographical center of the entire Spanish road network.
One of Madrid's absolute oldest dining spots, where locals know to stick to the casual downstairs bar to sip the house sherry.
A traditional market housed in a stunning 20th-century iron and glass structure, representing a brilliant piece of industrial architecture.
A peaceful square that connects with Madrid’s deep, diverse roots, representing three distinct architectural eras away from the crowds.
An intimate, low-lit Mediterranean dinner spot in La Latina known for fresh seafood and local produce, making it ideal for a date night.
A restaurant offering a tasting menu that is described as a 'mushroom utopia' for fungi lovers.
A restored, abandoned metro station from Madrid's early subway system, which trains still pass through. A museum to a bygone era of transport.
An Argentinian bakery and sit-down restaurant serving some of the most elaborate and decadent pastries in the city.
The cafe that made bagels a 'thing' in Madrid, serving them up for breakfast and brunch in the Chamberí neighborhood.
A massive, two-century-old botanical garden containing thousands of plant species and one of Europe's only desert environments in a greenhouse.
A laid-back bar in Lavapiés that nails vegan comfort food, serving plant-based versions of tortilla, burrata, and cakes.
A restaurant in Barrio de las Letras that successfully spotlights creative dishes from the Gran Canaria.
A popular croquetas bar in Malasaña that fills up fast; perfect for a cold beer and sharing plates of their famous croquettes.
A cozy Malasaña bookstore with soul, a resident cat, and shelves full of purpose, often hosting poetry readings and talks.
A chill, mostly vegan brunch joint and smoothie bar offering some of the more elaborate and beautiful breakfasts in Madrid.
A romantically ornate crystal palace in Retiro Park that has sheltered plants and art installations for over a century.
Located in Retiro Park, this is commonly acknowledged as the only public monument in the world dedicated to Lucifer.
A cozy corner supermarket in Salamanca offering authentic Italian products like fresh pasta, cheeses, wines, and gluten-free treats.
Recognized as the oldest restaurant in the world, this old-world Spanish eatery is still famous for its roast suckling pig.

name.en: Secret Nun Cookies at Monasterio del Corpus Christi

description.en: Order delicious cookies baked by cloistered nuns through a secret turntable at this 400-year-old monastery.
An iconic theatre offering a truly 'madrileño' experience with energetic storytelling, live orchestra, and traditional Spanish light opera.

prompt.en: Stand at the bottom of the street at 'golden hour' to shoot the neon and painted tavern signs lighting up against the twilight sky.
Take a low-angle shot looking up the famously narrow, historic street to emphasize its high concentration of taverns.
Frame the neo-Gothic spires against the sky using the branches of nearby trees to create a natural vignette.
Take a photo that highlights the mix of old and new, reflecting its construction from the 19th century to the 1990s.
Find the strangest, most obscure antique item in a shop window and photograph it from a low angle to make it look monumental.
Capture the chaotic, centuries-old maze of commerce and grit with a photo showing the density of stalls and shoppers.
Use a slow shutter speed to capture the chaotic motion blur of bartenders shouting orders over the crowd.
Take a wide shot that encapsulates the loud, crowded, and authentic Madrid tapas experience inside.
Capture a macro shot of the condensation on your chilled vermouth glass, using the elegant bar geometry as your blurred background.
Photograph the bar's interior, highlighting its understated elegance and genuinely cool, refined local atmosphere.
Shoot a cross-section of a slice of runny tortilla the exact moment the egg oozes onto the plate.
Capture a photo that represents the debate: runny vs. well-done tortilla. Maybe two different slices side-by-side.
Scavenger hunt: Photograph a vintage Metro logo or transit map in the immediate surrounding area of the headquarters.
Take a photo of the building at Plaza del Descubridor Diego de Ordás 3, the brain center for Europe's second-largest metro network.
Line up a 'flight' of different colored vermouths and shoot them backlit by the warm bar lighting.
Capture a photo that tells the story of exploring vermouth in all its forms, from fruity and sweet to herbaceous and bitter.
A close-up, highly detailed texture shot of their most unique, unusual tapa you won't find downtown.
Take a picture of the joint's exterior or interior that shows it's a highly localized spot for trying unique tapas.
Use burst mode to capture the exact moment your fork breaks the egg yolk and mixes it into the hot cheese of the Georgian bread bowl.
Photograph the bright, inviting Georgian restaurant's atmosphere, focusing on the incredible cheese-filled bread bowls.
Look straight up from the base of the tallest skyscraper to capture an imposing, abstract perspective of steel and glass converging in the sky.
Take a photo showing the stark contrast between the modern high-rise buildings and the city's old town architecture.
Find a puddle or reflective surface outside the stadium plaza to capture a mirrored shot of its massive exterior.
Capture the colossal scale of the 'ultimate football temple in Europe' from the outside, even without a game.
Look for a reflection of the sprawling steel-and-glass roof in the turtle pond.
Capture the surreal contrast between the massive indoor jungle and the bustling transit hub surrounding it.
Arrive exactly at 'blue hour' (post-sunset) to shoot the Gran Vía skyline facing west as the city lights turn on.
Take a picture of the iconic Minerva statue on the rooftop with the sprawling city of Madrid as its backdrop.
Find a famous literary quote inscribed in gold lettering on the cobblestones and photograph it with the blurred feet of locals walking past.
Capture the essence of the literary quarter's main artery, showing the architecture where Spain's Golden Age writers roamed.
A top-down 'flat lay' of your tapas spread, emphasizing the bright, vibrant colors of the curries against the market counter.
Take a photo that shows the restaurant stall inside the working Mercado de Antón Martín, capturing the fusion of fine dining and a market setting.
A tight, flash-free shot of a poured glass illuminating the deep red color using only the bar's ambient light.
Capture the entrance or exterior of this ultra under-the-radar wine bar, making it look like a secret discovery.
Capture the vintage signage or dusty, antique bottles lining the walls using only the moody, ambient lighting.
Take a photo that captures the authentic 'bullfighter’s dream bar' aesthetic, untouched by modern gentrification.
Capture the thick steam rising from a freshly opened bamboo dumpling basket, backlit by the restaurant's front window.
Photograph the cozy spot tucked just off Gran Vía, showing it as a beloved local hideout.
Find a vibrant piece of street art and wait for a pedestrian whose outfit accidentally matches it to walk into the frame.
Photograph the independent design shops and street art that define this avant-garde community thoroughfare.
Sit on a bench and use a slow shutter speed to capture the motion blur of local cyclists or skaters crossing the plaza.
Take a candid shot that captures the city's alternative youth culture, the ultimate spot for people-watching.
Photograph the illuminated ancient Egyptian temple reflecting perfectly in the surrounding water at dusk.
Capture the temple in a way that emphasizes the incredible historical oddity of an authentic Egyptian temple in the middle of Madrid.
Capture the movement of the drummers at an impromptu drum circle using a slow shutter speed on a summer evening.
Catch the golden evening sun hitting the monumental statue in El Retiro Park.
Photograph the bright, vibrant colors of the Persian breakfast spread in natural morning window light.
Capture the atmosphere of this unique spot, showing it serves local workers and residents, as it's only open for breakfast and lunch.
Photograph the striking window displays using reflections from the busy Calle Serrano traffic.
Take a photo of the flagship store's exterior that conveys its status as a genuine piece of Madrid's 19th-century high-fashion history.
The classic 'action shot'—capture the exact millisecond a churro is dunked into the thick, hot chocolate, catching the drip.
Take a close-up shot of the extra crispy, sugary churros, highlighting their texture before they meet the chocolate.
Stand safely on the pedestrian traffic island to get a perfectly symmetrical, head-on shot of the arches against the sky.
Take a photo that shows the massive scale of the neoclassical gate, a historic entrance to the city.
Capture the golden, crispy texture of the famous fried codfish right as the bartender drops it onto the counter.
Take a photo of the bar's definitively old-school interior, highlighting its authentic, no-frills atmosphere.
Get a drastic low-angle shot isolating the bear against the sky, strategically cropping out the surrounding crowds.
Take a photo of the iconic statue of the Bear and the Strawberry Tree that clearly shows it as the historic symbol of the city.
Photograph your tapas spread from a low angle, incorporating the bustling street outside Plaza Mayor as background bokeh.
Capture the exterior of this tapas spot, showing how it's hidden in plain sight near a highly touristy area.
Find the floor plaque in the massive plaza and take a 'shoe selfie' standing exactly on the center mark.
Take a wider shot that shows the small plaque in the context of the grand Puerta del Sol, marking the geographical center of Spain.
Frame a glass of amber house sherry against the ornate, 19th-century historic woodwork of the downstairs bar.
Capture the casual downstairs bar area, showing where locals go to sip sherry, away from the main tourist dining rooms.
Shoot the intricate ironwork framing the glass from the outside during the day, capturing the reflections of the old city in the panes.
Photograph the bustling interior, showing how the brilliant industrial architecture has been adapted for modern culinary life.
Take a picture of your tapas spread at a classic bar. The more variety, the better!
Find a beautifully worn, ancient-looking wooden door and take a selfie with it.
Get low and take an artistic shot of the cobblestone streets that define this historic neighborhood.
On a Sunday, capture the most unusual or interesting item you can find for sale at the El Rastro flea market.
Find a quiet plaza and capture a moment of local life: people chatting, kids playing, or someone enjoying a coffee.
Photograph an ornate wrought iron balcony, a classic feature of the Austrias architecture.
Take a photo of your drink, whether it's a caña (small beer), a vermouth, or a wine, at a local tavern.
Peer into an open doorway and snap a photo of a hidden interior courtyard or 'patio'.
Find a street sign with a historic or curious name and take a picture of it. Look up what it means!
Capture a building or architectural detail that you think best represents the Habsburg (Austrias) era.
Find a spot with a view (like from the viaduct or a rooftop bar) and capture the sea of red-tiled roofs.
This neighborhood is famous for tortilla de patatas. Find a slice and take a mouth-watering photo.
Play with the contrast of bright sun and deep shadows in one of the narrow, winding streets.
Find a quiet bench in a garden or plaza and take a photo of your feet up, enjoying a moment of rest.
Capture the facade of a traditional, family-run shop—a bakery, a butcher, or a craft store.
Find a residential building with a particularly beautiful or grand facade and capture its architectural details.
Take a selfie enjoying a coffee and a pastry at a local cafe, capturing the authentic neighborhood vibe.
Photograph a tree-lined street, showing how nature integrates with the urban, residential architecture.
Search for and photograph a building or detail that shows a modernist architectural influence.
Capture a scene of tranquility in a local park or plaza, away from the bustling city center.
Visit a local market like Mercado de Chamberí and take a photo of fresh produce or a local delicacy.
This is an elegant neighborhood. Snap a picture of a well-dressed local walking their equally stylish dog.
Find a bar or restaurant with a subtle, unassuming entrance and take an intriguing photo of it.
Many metro stations here have unique tilework or art. Capture an interesting detail from a local station.
Find a scene that shows a contrast between old architecture and modern life.
Take a photo of a classic building reflected in the window of a modern shop or office.
Photograph the tempting display of a local bakery, pastry shop, or delicatessen.
Order a 'tosta' (toast with toppings) for breakfast or a snack and get a delicious-looking shot.
Capture the intricate design of a grand entryway or 'portal' of an apartment building.
Take a selfie on a street that you feel best captures the sophisticated, local essence of Chamberí.
Find and photograph one of the famous literary quotes inscribed in gold on the pavement of Calle Huertas or surrounding streets.
Lavapiés is full of vibrant street art. Capture your favorite mural or graffiti piece.
Take a photo of a dish from one of the many international restaurants in Lavapiés (Indian, Senegalese, Moroccan, etc.).
Take a selfie in a cool, bohemian-style bar that captures the artistic vibe of the neighborhood.
Find a building with a brightly colored or uniquely decorated facade and snap a picture.
Capture the steep incline of one of Lavapiés's characteristic hilly streets.
Photograph the exterior of a historic tavern or building where a famous writer like Cervantes or Lope de Vega might have hung out.
Take a photo that shows the mix of different cultures living together in Lavapiés.
Find a small artisan shop—a luthier, a leather worker, a tailor—and photograph its storefront.
Capture the bustling energy inside the Mercado de Antón Martín or Mercado de San Fernando.
Photograph the poster or entrance of one of the many small, independent theaters in the area.
Take a photo of a book you're reading (or one from a bookshop) with a classic Barrio de las Letras street in the background.
Many residents decorate their balconies with plants. Find the most lush and beautiful balcony garden and snap a shot.
Capture a photo of a glass of vermouth on tap ('vermut de grifo'), a classic Madrid aperitif.
Take a candid (but respectful) photo that captures the diverse and interesting people of the neighborhood.
Photograph the most unique or quirky item you find in one of Malasaña's many vintage or second-hand shops.
Take a selfie that shows off your personal style against a backdrop that screams 'Malasaña' (like a cool mural or a punk bar).
Capture the lively, social atmosphere of the Plaza Dos de Mayo, the heart of the neighborhood.
Take an artistic photo of your coffee or craft beer at one of the neighborhood's trendy, independent cafes.
Instead of a big mural, find a small, clever piece of street art—a sticker, a small stencil, or a painted utility box.
Photograph the interior of a classic, old-school bodega or bar, focusing on the details like old bottles, barrels, or tilework.
Find and photograph the most creative or eye-catching storefront of an independent boutique, comic shop, or bookstore.
As evening falls, capture the glow of a cool neon sign from a bar or music venue.
Take a photo that you feel captures the counter-cultural, free-spirited energy of the 'Movida Madrileña' movement that was born here.
Get a picture of you and your friends (or just your drink) toasting on an outdoor terrace ('terraza').
Find a vinyl record store and take a picture of an album cover that catches your eye.
Photograph a unique snack or street food item you find in the neighborhood.
Find one of the old, ceramic street signs and take a close-up photo of it.
Take a photo from your point of view while sitting on a bench or at a cafe, capturing the flow of interesting people passing by.
Find a scene that contrasts old, weathered brick with modern, sleek materials or vibrant paint.
Take a photo of the most extravagant or artistic window display you can find on Calle Serrano or Calle de Velázquez.
Treat yourself to a fancy pastry from a high-end 'pastelería' and take a picture before you indulge.
Capture the facade of a stately 19th-century apartment building, focusing on its ornate balconies and grand entrance.
Take a selfie relaxing by the lake, in a rose garden, or under a beautiful tree in Retiro Park.
Take a close-up shot of a famous designer logo on a storefront or a shopping bag.
Photograph the beautifully set table or chic interior of a gourmet restaurant or cafe.
Find an art gallery and take a picture of a piece of art that catches your eye through the window.
Take a photo of your shoes as you walk along the 'Milla de Oro' (Golden Mile), the epicenter of luxury shopping.
Spot and snap a picture of a particularly well-groomed and stylish dog, likely accompanying its equally fashionable owner.
Find a chic bar and take a photo of a glass of cava or champagne.
Explore the side streets to find a quieter, charming alley or 'callejón' hidden away from the main avenues.
Visit a gourmet market like Mercado de la Paz and photograph an exotic fruit, a fine cheese, or a leg of jamón ibérico.
The grid-like layout of Salamanca is perfect for this. Find a street and take a perfectly symmetrical photo looking down its length.
Capture the reflection of an elegant building in a puddle, a car window, or a shopfront.
Take a selfie where you look your most sophisticated, with a suitably elegant Salamanca backdrop.
Take a selfie at Kilometre Zero in Puerta del Sol, the official starting point for all of Spain's national roads.
Grab a classic 'bocadillo de calamares' near Plaza Mayor and take a delicious-looking photo before your first bite.
Find and photograph a shop that has been open for over 100 years. Look for traditional wooden or tiled facades.
Get to a rooftop bar or viewpoint and capture the stunning view over the rooftops of central Madrid.
Take a panoramic photo that captures the grandeur and scale of the Plaza Mayor.
Capture a photo of one of the many street performers or costumed characters in Puerta del Sol or near the Royal Palace.
Photograph a colorful tapa or pintxo from the bustling Mercado de San Miguel.
Take a photo of the Royal Palace reflected in a puddle or a window.
Photograph the iconic Tío Pepe or Schweppes neon sign in Puerta del Sol or Plaza de Callao.
Even in the busiest area, find a quiet side street or hidden plaza and capture a moment of peace.
Photograph some 'turrón' from a traditional sweet shop or 'churros con chocolate'.
Find a statue in a plaza and mimic its pose for a fun photo.
Use a slow shutter speed (if your phone has the option) or just capture the motion of the crowds on a busy street like Gran Vía or Calle Preciados.
Find a shop selling traditional Spanish fans ('abanicos') and take a colorful photo of the display.
Stand in the middle of a plaza and take a photo looking straight up at the sky, framed by the surrounding historic buildings.
